name: M.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The program deliberately blends business fundamentals (ACCT 615, FIN 600, HRM 601, EM 636/637 project management) with a deep IS/CS technical pool (databases CS 631/632/731, analytics/ML CS 634/636/675/732, security CS 608/645/647/708), which is appropriate for a business-plus-IS degree and shows genuine graduate depth (deepestPrereqChain=3). However, with requirementGroups=0 and 85 referenced courses there is no visible required core or named concentration, so the 40-course sample reads as an undifferentiated elective sprawl (healthcare ergonomics IE 686-688, GIS CE 602, wireless security CS 755) with no clear progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a required core plus 2-3 named concentrations (e.g., business analytics, IT management, secure systems) so the large elective pool has structure and a clear path.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy and shows real graduate depth: deepestPrereqChain=3 with clean chains (CS 631->CS 632, CS 645->CS 647, CS 608/CS 645/CS 696->CS 708) and missingCourseCount=0 with no dangling references.
dimensions.suggestion: Preserve the depth but clean prerequisite fields where course-description text has merged in (CS 696, IS 631, IS 665) so entry requirements are unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, so the business-and-IS competencies the M.S. certifies are not stated for a degree that spans two disciplines.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes explicitly bridging business decision-making and information-systems technical skills.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: For a full M.S. degree, statedTotalCredits is null and requirementGroups=0, so neither the total (typically ~30 credits) nor the split among business core, IS/technical core, and electives is transparent; creditReconcileDelta is null so internal consistency cannot be checked.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and how credits divide among business core, IS core, and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Individual course descriptions are strong (FIN 600 on capital budgeting; CS 708 on data security/privacy paradigms), but as a degree page it is incomplete: no plan of study, no term labeling, no credit total, and an 85-course reference list with no core/elective distinction leaves a prospective student unable to see how to actually complete the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the entry with a labeled plan of study, credit summary, and a curated course list organized by core versus concentration.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The technical electives are current and marketable: big data (CS 644), machine learning (CS 675/732), data mining (CS 634), R analytics (CS 636), cloud (CS 652), and modern security (CS 645/647/708), paired with solid business courses. The gap is a business-analytics/decision capstone and generative-AI-for-business content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a business-analytics decision capstone and a generative-AI-for-business elective so the degree tracks how firms now deploy AI and data.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across both halves of the program (ACCT 615 on managerial-accounting methods; CS 732 on regularization and ensemble learning); none are thin or placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are internally consistent with no dangling references, but many technical courses have no stated prerequisite and several fields blend description text into the prerequisite (CS 696, IS 631, IS 665), so clarity is uneven.

missingTopics.rationale: Data mining (CS 634) and ML (CS 675) teach methods, but no integrative course applies analytics to business decision-making end to end.
The degree has technical and business courses but no course connecting IS investment to competitive strategy and organizational change.
CS 708 covers technical data security, but governance, regulatory compliance (GDPR/CCPA), and data-stewardship for business are absent.
Enterprise database management (IS 631) is present, but ERP/enterprise-application integration central to business IS is not represented.

missingTopics.topic: Product management and agile business analysis
missingTopics.rationale: The program lacks a bridge between business stakeholders and technical delivery (product/roadmap/backlog practices).

emergingTrends.rationale: Cognitive computing (CS 676) and ML (CS 675) provide a base to add LLM-driven decision support and automation relevant to enterprises.
CS 644 (big data) could be extended to pipelines, warehousing, and self-service analytics that businesses now run on.
Pairing FIN 600 with CS 634/CS 675 positions graduates for data-driven finance and risk analytics.

emergingTrends.trend: Responsible and governed AI in enterprises
emergingTrends.rationale: As firms deploy models, AI governance, fairness, and auditability become required competencies the degree can add.

emergingTrends.trend: Cloud-based business platforms and SaaS economics
emergingTrends.rationale: Extending CS 652 (cognitive cloud) toward platform strategy connects the technical and business halves.

topSuggestions: Publish a required core plus named concentrations and the total credit requirement (~30) so the 85-course pool becomes a navigable degree.
Add a business/decision-analytics capstone that integrates the technical methods with business problem-solving.
Add generative-AI-for-business and data-governance/compliance electives to close the most important currency gaps.
Publish 3-5 program-level learning outcomes bridging business and IS competencies.
Rebuild the catalog page with a labeled plan of study and credit summary.
